ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers moussaka as a general category; the 'mixed' variant served at Sultan Palace is a restaurant-specific preparation combining lamb, beef, and vegetables. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers moussaka as a dish category. The mixed variant with multiple meat types is not separately coded; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for moussaka or mixed moussaka as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of mixed moussaka is approximately 350 g, comprising layered eggplant, mixed ground meat, and béchamel topping as served at a Turkish restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Moussaka, with meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 22904) and FAO/INFOODS Middle Eastern Food Composition Table, adapted for Turkish mixed-meat variant
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for moussaka with meat (NDB 22904 / FDC ID 169238) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values represent a standard moussaka with ground meat and béchamel. The 'mixed' designation at Sultan Palace indicates a combination of lamb and beef rather than a single meat; nutrient profile is consistent with this composition. Minor adjustments to sodium reflect restaurant-level seasoning. FAO/INFOODS Middle Eastern composition data used as cross-reference for eggplant-based baked dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed dishes and composite meals — moussaka analogous estimate based on eggplant-meat-béchamel composition; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for moussaka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the dish composition: eggplant (GI ~15), ground meat (GI negligible), béchamel sauce with wheat flour (GI ~50), tomato (GI ~15). Weighted average by carbohydrate contribution yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 35. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (35 × 9.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 3.5) / 100 = 11.2 g. This estimation methodology follows Brand-Miller et al. (2008) composite meal GI principles.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320 mg per 100 g; a 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium load from a full restaurant serving can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated low glycemic index of 35 is favourable, but the glycemic load per full serving (~11 g) and the moderate carbohydrate content from béchamel and tomato sauce require portion awareness.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (150–200 g) of mixed moussaka as part of a balanced meal, given its low estimated GI, but should monitor total carbohydrate intake from the béchamel component and avoid pairing with additional high-GI starchy sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 3.9 g per 100 g and cholesterol of approximately 52 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g serving provides approximately 13.7 g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit for cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit moussaka to a half-portion (approximately 175 g) and prefer preparations using leaner ground meat and reduced-fat béchamel to lower saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Moussaka (Turkish: Musakka) is a classic baked casserole dish of Turkish and broader Eastern Mediterranean origin, consisting of layered sliced eggplant (aubergine), a mixture of seasoned ground lamb and beef, tomato-onion sauce spiced with cinnamon and allspice, and a creamy béchamel topping made from wheat flour, butter, milk, and egg. The dish is baked in the oven until the béchamel is golden and set. The 'mixed' designation at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore refers to the use of both lamb and beef mince, distinguishing it from single-meat versions. The texture is layered and hearty: soft eggplant, savoury spiced meat, and a rich creamy topping. The flavour profile is warm, mildly spiced, and umami-rich. Nutritionally, mixed moussaka provides moderate protein (~8.5 g/100 g) from the meat, moderate fat (~9.8 g/100 g) including saturated fat from the béchamel and meat, and a relatively low carbohydrate content (~9.2 g/100 g) primarily from the béchamel sauce and tomato. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), making it more suitable for blood glucose management than many other baked pasta or grain-based dishes. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 320 mg/100 g due to seasoning and cheese, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is a good source of iron, zinc, and B vitamins from the meat component, and provides calcium from the béchamel and cheese. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or dairy allergies. In Singapore, this dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine at Boat Quay, priced at SGD 27.90 per portion.
common_user_queries: What is Turkish moussaka made of?
What is the difference between Greek and Turkish moussaka?
Where can I eat moussaka in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact match for mixed moussaka found in a primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy moussaka with meat (FDC ID 169238) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Middle Eastern composition data. GI is an analogous estimate based on component GI values and Brand-Miller composite meal methodology. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is a well-characterised moussaka entry and the adaptation is minor.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for moussaka. 2. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for moussaka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. The estimation follows Brand-Miller et al. (2008) composite meal GI principles using component GI values weighted by carbohydrate contribution. 3. The 'mixed' designation refers to a combination of ground lamb and ground beef as used at Sultan Palace, Boat Quay, Singapore. 4. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's Turkish and Lebanese cuisine context; formal halal certification has not been independently verified. 5. Sodium value of 320 mg/100 g reflects a restaurant-level preparation with seasoning; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. Turkish cuisine does not require Singaporean mandatory language coverage per G6, but names are provided for LLM retrieval utility given the Singapore serving context. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code for 'mixed moussaka' exists.

Sourced nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169238 (moussaka with meat) as primary approved source.
Estimated GI at 35 using Brand-Miller et al. (2008) composite meal methodology with component GI values; documented fully in gi_source.

Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
